author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Original Sin Does Not Render Marriage Evil.
title: Original Sin Does Not Render Marriage Evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 38 [XXXIII.]—Original Sin Does Not Render Marriage Evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But they argue thus, saying: “Is not, then, marriage an evil, and the man that is produced by marriage not God’s work?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: As if the good of the married life were that disease of concupiscence with which they who know not God love their wives—a course which the apostle forbids; and not rather that conjugal chastity, by which carnal lust is reduced to the good purposes of the appointed procreation of children.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Or as if, forsooth, a man could possibly be anything but God’s work, not only when born in wedlock, but even if he be produced in fornication or adultery.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: In the present inquiry, however, when the question is not for what a Creator is necessary, but for what a Saviour, we have not to consider what good there is in the procreation of nature, but what evil there is in sin, whereby our nature has been certainly corrupted.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: No doubt the two are generated simultaneously—both nature and nature’s corruption; one of which is good, the other evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The one comes to us from the bounty of the Creator, the other is contracted from the condemnation of our origin; the one has its cause in the good-will of the Supreme God, the other in the depraved will of the first man; the one exhibits God as the maker of the creature, the other exhibits God as the punisher of disobedience: in short, the very same Christ was the maker of man for the creation of the one, and was mademan for the healing of the other.
